items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.title: Streamlining DAA Treatment for HCV: Key Strategies and Outcomes
items.content: To meet global and national goals for reducing HCV prevalence, efforts focus on enhancing screening, diagnosis, and treatment enrollment. Simplified DAA regimens allow most patients to avoid in-person specialist care unless they have complex conditions. Successful streamlining strategies include telehealth programs, one-stop screening and treatment initiation, minimal monitoring approaches, and collaborative teams addressing substance use disorders.
keyPoints: WHO goal: 90% reduction in global HCV prevalence by 2030
U.S. goal: 80% reduction in HCV prevalence by 2030
Simplified DAA regimens reduce need for specialist care, except in complex cases
Successful strategies: telehealth, one-stop screening/treatment, minimal monitoring, collaborative teams
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.title: Defining Autoimmune Hepatitis (AIH) and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Autoimmune hepatitis is a chronic condition marked by hepatocellular necrosis, inflammation, and potential progression to cirrhosis. Survival rates are significantly higher with treatment (80-98% over 10 years) compared to untreated cases (67%). AIH predominantly affects women (~75%) and shows a bimodal age distribution. Extrahepatic autoimmunity features support its autoimmune nature.
keyPoints: AIH: chronic disorder with hepatocellular necrosis, inflammation, fibrosis
10-year survival: 80-98% treated vs. 67% untreated
Female predominance in AIH cases (~75%)
Bimodal age distribution: peaks <30 and >60 years
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.title: AIH Pathogenesis: Cell-Mediated Immune Attack and Genetic Predisposition
items.content: Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is characterized by a cell-mediated immunologic attack against liver cells, often triggered by environmental factors in genetically predisposed individuals. This process involves cytotoxic T cells and plasma cells infiltrating the liver.
keyPoints: Cell-mediated immune response targets liver cells due to loss of tolerance for self-liver antigens.
Genetic predisposition is likely inherited; liver specificity may be triggered by environmental factors (e.g., drugs, viruses).
Histopathologic lesions in AIH are predominantly composed of cytotoxic T cells and plasma cells.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.title: Genetic Associations and Autoantibodies in AIH
items.content: AIH is associated with specific histocompatibility haplotypes and the presence of circulating autoantibodies. These genetic markers and antibodies are common among patients and their relatives.
keyPoints: Increased frequency of other autoimmune disorders in AIH patients and their relatives (e.g., thyroiditis, rheumatoid arthritis).
Common histocompatibility haplotypes: HLA-B1, B8, DR3, DR4; extended haplotype alleles: DRB1*0301, DRB1*0401.
Circulating autoantibodies in AIH include ANAs and ASMAs.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.title: Role of Humoral Immunity in Extrahepatic Manifestations of AIH
items.content: Humoral immune mechanisms contribute to extrahepatic manifestations in AIH, such as arthralgias and vasculitis, through the deposition of circulating immune complexes.
keyPoints: Extrahepatic symptoms (e.g., arthritis, cutaneous vasculitis) are mediated by immune complex deposition.
Complement activation follows immune complex deposition, leading to inflammation and tissue injury.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.title: Fatigue as a Common Symptom of AIH
items.content: Fatigue is the most common symptom in autoimmune hepatitis (AIH), occurring in approximately 85% of patients. This symptom can be confused with acute viral hepatitis due to its insidious or abrupt onset.
keyPoints: Fatigue occurs in ~85% of AIH patients
Symptom may mimic acute viral hepatitis
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Severe Disease Presentation and Mortality Risk
items.content: In severe cases of AIH, characterized by aminotransferase levels >10 times normal and marked hyperglobulinemia, the mortality rate without therapy can reach up to 40% within six months. These cases often present with aggressive histologic lesions such as bridging necrosis or multilobular collapse.
keyPoints: Severe AIH: aminotransferase levels >10 times normal
Mortality rate without therapy can be up to 40% in 6 months
Aggressive histologic lesions include bridging necrosis and multilobular collapse
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Laboratory Features of AIH
items.content: Liver biochemical tests in AIH are invariably abnormal but may not correlate with clinical severity or histopathologic features. Common findings include elevated serum AST and ALT levels, which can range from 100 to 1000 units. In severe cases, serum bilirubin is moderately elevated (51–171 μmol/L [3–10 mg/dL]), and hypoalbuminemia occurs in very active or advanced disease.
keyPoints: Liver biochemical tests are invariably abnormal
Serum AST and ALT levels range from 100 to 1000 units
Severe cases: serum bilirubin moderately elevated (51–171 μmol/L [3–10 mg/dL])
Hypoalbuminemia in very active or advanced disease
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Autoantibodies in Autoimmune Hepatitis
items.content: In autoimmune hepatitis (AIH), specific autoantibodies help differentiate between Type I and Type II AIH. Type I is associated with ANAs, ASMA, and SLA antibodies, while Type II involves anti-LKM1 antibodies.
keyPoints: Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia (>2.5 g/dL) common in AIH
Type I AIH: ANAs (homogeneous pattern), ASMA (63% specificity with ANA), SLA antibodies associated with worse prognosis
Type II AIH: anti-LKM1, linked to HLA-DRB1 and HLA-DQB1 haplotypes
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Histological Features of Autoimmune Hepatitis
items.content: Liver biopsy is crucial for diagnosing AIH, revealing interface hepatitis, plasma cells in infiltrates, and central vein perivenulitis. These features distinguish AIH from other liver diseases.
keyPoints: Interface hepatitis: inflammation beyond portal tracts
Plasma cells presence in inflammatory infiltrate
Central vein perivenulitis characteristic of AIH
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Establishing a Diagnosis of Autoimmune Hepatitis (AIH)
items.content: To diagnose AIH, exclude other liver diseases and use criteria like hyperglobulinemia, autoantibodies, and histologic features. A diagnostic scoring system considers factors such as gender, enzyme levels, autoantibodies, concurrent autoimmune diseases, specific HLA markers, and treatment response.
keyPoints: Exclude genetic disorders, viral hepatitis, drug hepatotoxicity, alcohol

Factors favoring AIH diagnosis: female gender, aminotransferase elevation, globulin level, nuclear/antibodies (smooth-muscle, LKM1), other autoimmune diseases, histologic features (interface hepatitis, plasma cells, rosettes), HLA-DR3 or DR4 markers, treatment response
Simplified scoring: autoantibodies (ANA, ASMA, anti-SLA/LKM), elevated serum IgG, compatible histology, absence of viral hepatitis markers
Aminotransferase elevation required for diagnosis
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis in Chronic Hepatitis
items.content: AIH can mimic acute viral hepatitis or drug-induced liver injury early on. Exclude viral hepatitis with serologic tests. Wilson disease, cryptogenic cirrhosis, primary biliary cholangitis, and MASH may present similarly to AIH but are distinguishable by specific clinical, biochemical, serologic, and histologic assessments.
keyPoints: Exclude acute viral hepatitis early in chronic hepatitis
Wilson disease: serum ceruloplasmin, serum/urinary copper levels, liver copper measurement
Distinguish from cryptogenic cirrhosis, primary biliary cholangitis, MASH using historic/biochemical/serologic/histologic assessments
Differentiate autoimmune from chronic viral hepatitis, especially with overlapping antibodies
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Overlap Syndromes and ICI Hepatitis in AIH Diagnosis
items.content: AIH can overlap with autoimmune biliary disorders like primary biliary cholangitis or sclerosing cholangitis. Immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) hepatitis resembles AIH but lacks female preponderance, often has low-titer ANA, and may not show plasma cell infiltrates histologically.
keyPoints: Overlap syndromes with autoimmune biliary disorders
ICIs can cause liver-targeted autoimmunity resembling AIH without female preponderance
ICI hepatitis: less likely to have ANA; if present, low-titer and no plasma cell infiltrates
Response to therapy may distinguish between overlap syndromes
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Glucocorticoid Therapy in Autoimmune Hepatitis
items.content: Glucocorticoids are the cornerstone of treatment for autoimmune hepatitis, inducing remission in up to 80% of patients. The therapeutic response is defined by a >50% reduction in ALT within 4 weeks.
keyPoints: Therapeutic response expected in up to 80% of patients
>50% reduction in ALT by 4 weeks defines response
Prednisone favored over prednisolone, with initial doses of 60 mg/d in the US or 20 mg in Europe
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.title: Steroid-Sparing Agents and Maintenance Therapy
items.content: After an early response to corticosteroids, azathioprine is introduced as a steroid-sparing agent. It helps reduce complications from steroids over 18 months and allows further tapering of prednisone.
keyPoints: Azathioprine initiated 7–14 days after corticosteroid response
Dosing: 50-150 mg, typically 1–2 mg/kg per day for maintenance
Reduction in steroid complications from 66% to <20% over 18 months
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.title: Relapse and Long-term Management of Autoimmune Hepatitis
items.content: Biochemical remission requires continued therapy for at least 24 months. Relapse is common, with a rate of 81% after 3 years, necessitating indefinite treatment in most cases.
keyPoints: Therapy continues for at least 24 months post-remission
Relapse rate is at least 50%, and 81% after 3 years
95% of patients require indefinite maintenance therapy
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.title: Management of Refractory Autoimmune Hepatitis
items.content: In cases where autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is refractory to standard treatments like azathioprine, 6-MP, MMF, or prednisone, alternative therapies such as tacrolimus and rituximab can be considered. Tacrolimus has shown effectiveness in reducing aminotransferase levels in up to 80% of patients after 3 months but requires careful monitoring due to nephrotoxicity risk. Rituximab, particularly effective in refractory AIH, achieves high remission rates and allows for corticosteroid dose reduction.
keyPoints: Tacrolimus can reduce aminotransferase levels in up to 80% of patients after 3 months
Rituximab (1000-mg IV infusion twice, 2 weeks apart) achieves remission rates of 95% at 1 year and 71% at 2 years
Tacrolimus requires careful trough monitoring due to nephrotoxicity risk
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.title: Liver Transplantation in Severe Autoimmune Hepatitis
items.content: For patients with severe AIH refractory to high-dose corticosteroids or those progressing to cirrhosis and end-stage liver disease, liver transplantation is the definitive treatment. If bilirubin levels do not improve after 2 weeks of therapy, early consideration for transplantation should be made. Although recurrence in the new liver occurs rarely, it can happen in up to 35-40% of cases; however, both patient and graft survival rates exceed 80% at 5 years.
keyPoints: Liver transplantation is indicated when AIH progresses to cirrhosis or end-stage liver disease
Bilirubin failure to improve after 2 weeks should prompt consideration for transplantation
Recurrence of AIH in the new liver occurs in up to 35-40% of cases, but 5-year survival rates exceed 80%
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.question: What is the minimum duration for which hepatic inflammation and necrosis must persist to be classified as chronic hepatitis?
items.explanation: Chronic hepatitis is defined by the persistence of hepatic inflammation and necrosis for at least 6 months.

options: Classification based solely on clinical features
Classification based solely on serologic markers
A combination of clinical, serologic, and histologic variables

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of chronic viral hepatitis?
items.explanation: Chronic viral hepatitis can be caused by Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, and autoimmune hepatitis; Hepatitis E is not listed as a cause.

items.question: Which histologic feature refers to the disruption of the limiting plate of periportal hepatocytes by inflammatory cells?
items.explanation: The disruption of the limiting plate of periportal hepatocytes by inflammatory cells is referred to as piecemeal necrosis or interface hepatitis.

items.question: Which scoring system for viral hepatitis is commonly used in Europe?
items.explanation: The METAVIR score is the most popular scoring system for viral hepatitis used in Europe.

items.question: Which scoring system categorizes the degree of hepatic fibrosis on a scale from 0 to 6?
items.explanation: The HAI (Histological Activity Index) categorizes the degree of hepatic fibrosis on a scale from 0 to 6.

items.question: Which form of viral hepatitis is typically self-limited and does not cause chronic hepatitis?
items.explanation: Hepatitis A is typically self-limited and does not cause chronic hepatitis.

items.question: What noninvasive approach uses serum biomarkers to approximate hepatic histologic stage?
items.explanation: Noninvasive approaches such as imaging determinations of liver elasticity/stiffness, along with serum biomarkers like those in the ELF score, are used to approximate hepatic histologic stage.

items.question: What is the likelihood of chronic infection after acute hepatitis B when the infection occurs at birth?
items.explanation: Infection at birth is associated with a 90% chance of chronic infection.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.question: What is the approximate 5-year survival rate for patients with mild chronic hepatitis B?
items.explanation: The 5-year survival rate for patients with mild chronic hepatitis is 97%.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.question: Which factor does not accelerate the progression of hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Older age is not mentioned as a factor that accelerates the progression of hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.question: Which phase of HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B is characterized by high HBV DNA levels and significant liver injury?
items.explanation: The replicative phase is characterized by high HBV DNA levels and significant liver injury.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.question: In patients with HBeAg-positive chronic HBV infection acquired in early childhood, what is the relationship between viral replication and liver injury during the initial decades?
options: High levels of viral replication correlate with significant liver injury.
Negligible levels of liver injury despite very high levels of viral replication.
Liver injury correlates directly with decreasing levels of viral replication.
There is no correlation between viral replication and liver injury.
items.explanation: In early decades, patients have high HBV replication but negligible liver injury due to host immunologic tolerance.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.question: Which statement best describes the risk of late-life complications in patients with childhood-acquired HBV infection?
options: They have a decreased risk for cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
The risk is confined mostly to persons younger than age 40.
Activation of liver injury emerges as tolerance declines, increasing the risk for late-life complications.
There is no increased risk later in life compared to those with adult-acquired HBV.
items.explanation: As host tolerance declines, patients are at increased risk for cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma later in life.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.question: What distinguishes HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B from HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B regarding HBV DNA levels?
options: HBeAg-negative cases have higher HBV DNA levels.
There is no difference in HBV DNA levels between the two types.
HBeAg-negative cases have several orders of magnitude lower HBV DNA levels.
HBeAg-positive cases cannot be detected by standard virologic tests.
items.explanation: HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B has significantly lower HBV DNA levels compared to HBeAg-positive cases.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.question: What is the most important risk factor for developing cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma in chronic hepatitis B patients?
options: The age at which infection was acquired.
The geographical region where the infection occurred.
items.explanation: The level of HBV replication is the most important risk factor for cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma in both HBeAg-reactive and HBeAg-negative patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B
items.question: Which of the following laboratory features is distinctly absent in chronic hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Hyperglobulinemia and detectable circulating autoantibodies are distinctly absent in chronic hepatitis B, unlike autoimmune hepatitis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B
items.question: Which of the following is a first-line agent recommended for treating chronic hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Entecavir is recommended as a first-line agent for treating chronic hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B
items.question: In severe cases of chronic hepatitis B, which laboratory finding is typically observed?
items.explanation: In severe cases, once cirrhosis is established, AST tends to exceed ALT.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B

items.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes the efficacy of standard IFN therapy in immunocompetent adults with HBeAg-reactive chronic hepatitis B?
options: A loss of HBeAg and HBV DNA reduction to levels below 10^5–10^6 virions/mL occurred in ~70% of patients.
Seroconversion from HBeAg to anti-HBe occurred in ~30% of patients.
A 16-week course of subcutaneous IFN resulted in a loss of HBeAg and HBV DNA reduction below 10^5–10^6 virions/mL in ~30% of patients.
Relapse after successful therapy was common, occurring in over 50% of patients.
items.explanation: Standard IFN therapy resulted in a loss of HBeAg and HBV DNA reduction below 10^5–10^6 virions/mL in ~30% of immunocompetent adults with HBeAg-reactive chronic hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which group of patients did NOT respond well to standard IFN therapy for chronic hepatitis B?
options: Immunocompetent adults with high-level HBV DNA.
Persons with neonatal acquisition of infection and minimal-to-mild ALT elevations.
Patients with decompensated chronic hepatitis B.
All patients responded equally well to standard IFN therapy.
items.explanation: Standard IFN therapy was not effective in immunosuppressed persons, those with neonatal acquisition of infection and minimal-to-mild ALT elevations, or patients with decompensated chronic hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: What was a common side effect of IFN therapy that could be reversible upon dose lowering or cessation?
items.explanation: 'Flu-like' symptoms were a common reversible side effect of IFN therapy upon dose lowering or cessation.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: What was the impact of successful antiviral therapy with IFN on the long-term outcomes for chronic hepatitis B patients?
options: No significant change in long-term survival or HCC frequency.
Increased risk of severe adverse effects and decompensation.
Improved long-term and complication-free survival, and reduced frequency of HCC.
Worsened liver histology despite antiviral therapy.
items.explanation: Successful antiviral therapy with IFN improved long-term and complication-free survival as well as reduced the frequency of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: What was the approximate rate of HBeAg seroconversion in patients with ALT levels ≥5× the upper limit of normal after one year of lamivudine therapy?
items.explanation: Patients with ALT levels ≥5× the upper limit of normal could expect 1-year HBeAg seroconversion rates of 50–60%.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: What was a common outcome in lamivudine-treated patients after discontinuing therapy for one year?
items.explanation: After discontinuing therapy for one year, two- to threefold ALT elevations occurred in 20–30% of lamivudine-treated patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: What mutation is associated with long-term monotherapy using lamivudine?
items.explanation: Long-term monotherapy with lamivudine was associated with methionine-to-valine (M204V) or methionine-to-isoleucine (M204I) mutations.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: What was the recommended duration of consolidation therapy after HBeAg seroconversion in Western patients?
items.explanation: A period of consolidation therapy of ≥6 months was recommended for Western patients after HBeAg seroconversion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which antiviral agent was shown to reduce the risk of progression to hepatic decompensation and HCC in patients with cirrhosis or advanced fibrosis?
items.explanation: Lamivudine was effective in reducing the risk of progression to hepatic decompensation and HCC.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: What is contraindicated for patients with HIV infection who are being treated for chronic hepatitis B?
options: Lamivudine monotherapy at the HBV daily dose of 100 mg
items.explanation: Lamivudine monotherapy at the HBV daily dose of 100 mg is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: Which antiviral agent was less prone to resistance compared to lamivudine and effective against YMDD-mutant HBV?
items.explanation: Adefovir was less prone to resistance than lamivudine and effective against YMDD-mutant HBV.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: What is a common outcome for HBeAg-negative patients when adefovir therapy is discontinued?
options: Sustained suppression of HBV DNA without reactivation
items.explanation: In HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B, reactivation is the rule when adefovir therapy is discontinued.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: What was the median reduction in HBV DNA levels observed with telbivudine treatment for HBeAg-reactive disease?
items.explanation: Telbivudine showed a median reduction of 6.4 $ \log_{10} $ in HBV DNA for HBeAg-reactive disease.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 4)

items.question: Which mutation is associated with resistance to telbivudine?
items.explanation: Resistance to telbivudine is associated with the M204I mutation.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 4)

items.question: What percentage of HBeAg-negative patients had suppressed HBV DNA levels to <300 copies/mL in telbivudine's registration trial?
items.explanation: In the registration trial, 88% of HBeAg-negative patients had suppressed HBV DNA levels to <300 copies/mL.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 4)

items.question: Which PEG IFN preparation was evaluated for chronic hepatitis B treatment after demonstrating effectiveness in hepatitis C?
items.explanation: Long-acting PEG IFN was shown to be effective in hepatitis C treatment before being evaluated for chronic hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.question: In the study involving PEG IFN-α2b, what was the weekly dosage administered during the first 32 weeks?
items.explanation: PEG IFN-α2b was administered at a dosage of 100 μg weekly for the first 32 weeks.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.question: What percentage range of HBeAg loss occurred in PEG IFN monotherapy arms at the end of therapy?
items.explanation: HBeAg loss occurred in approximately 22–27% in PEG IFN monotherapy arms at the end of therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which HBV genotype is associated with the highest likelihood of HBeAg loss when treated with PEG IFN-α2b?
items.explanation: HBV genotype A is associated with the highest likelihood of HBeAg loss in PEG IFN-α2b treated patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment was shown to be superior to PEG IFN-α2a in HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B at the end of therapy according to a trial?
items.explanation: Both lamivudine monotherapy and combination lamivudine-PEG IFN were superior to PEG IFN at the end of therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients maintained HBV DNA suppression to <400 copies/mL up to 5 years after initial treatment with PEG IFN in HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Among patients followed for up to 5 years, 17% maintained HBV DNA suppression.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended action if HBsAg levels fail to fall within the first 12–24 weeks of PEG IFN therapy in chronic hepatitis B?
items.explanation: PEG IFN therapy should be discontinued if HBsAg levels do not fall within the first 12–24 weeks.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.question: In HBeAg-positive patients, what was the cumulative percentage of undetectable HBV DNA at 96 weeks with entecavir treatment?
items.explanation: At 96 weeks, 80% of patients treated with entecavir had undetectable HBV DNA.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which antiviral medication is not recommended as a first-line treatment for lamivudine-resistant hepatitis B due to increasing resistance over time?
items.explanation: Entecavir resistance increases over time, making it less attractive for lamivudine-resistant hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which medication has been shown to have a higher rate of HBeAg seroconversion in HBeAg-positive patients after 48 weeks?
items.explanation: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate shows a higher rate of HBeAg seroconversion (8%) compared to entecavir (3%).
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which drug is associated with reduced renal and bone toxicity due to its targeted delivery to hepatocytes?
items.explanation: Tenofovir alafenamide reduces systemic exposure, minimizing renal and bone toxicity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which medication is recommended for patients with lamivudine-resistant hepatitis B due to its high barrier to resistance?
items.explanation: Tenofovir is recommended due to its high barrier to resistance and effectiveness against lamivudine-resistant HBV.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which antiviral agent is recommended over TDF by AASLD and EASL for patients with reduced renal function?
items.explanation: TAF is preferred due to its advantages in maintaining renal function.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.question: What was the observed reduction in median bone density at the hip for patients treated with TDF compared to those treated with TAF?
items.explanation: TAF showed a smaller reduction in bone density compared to TDF.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.question: What was the percentage of patients achieving HBV DNA reductions to <29 IU/mL with TAF treatment?
items.explanation: 94% of individuals treated with TAF achieved this reduction.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which statement is true regarding resistance to antiviral agents in the trial?
options: Resistance emerged to both TAF and TDF
Resistance did not emerge to either TAF or TDF
items.explanation: No resistance was observed for either TAF or TDF throughout the trial.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.question: Which combination therapy is approved for HIV but not yet approved for hepatitis B?
items.explanation: The combination of tenofovir and emtricitabine is approved for HIV but not yet approved for hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.question: What is a potential role of the TDF/FTC combination in patients with chronic hepatitis B?
options: It may be used as preexposure prophylaxis for HIV.
It is effective against lamivudine-resistant disease.
It eradicates cccDNA in all cases.
items.explanation: TDF/FTC may have a role in preventing HIV infection as preexposure prophylaxis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.question: What is the main concern when discontinuing TDF/FTC therapy in patients with chronic hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Discontinuing TDF/FTC therapy can lead to virologic and clinical reactivation of hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.question: Which drug has been approved in the European Union for HBV/HDV co-infection but not for HBV mono-infection?
items.explanation: Bulevirtide is approved in the EU for HBV/HDV co-infection but not for HBV mono-infection.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.question: Which patients with chronic hepatitis B are not recommended for treatment according to the passage?
options: HBeAg-positive patients with ALT levels >2× the upper limit of normal
Patients with compensated cirrhosis and detectable HBV DNA
HBeAg-negative patients with ALT >2x the upper limit of normal
items.explanation: No treatment is recommended for inactive 'nonreplicative' hepatitis B carriers.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.question: For HBeAg-positive patients with ALT ≤2× the upper limit of normal, what does current recommendation suggest?
options: Treatment only if HBV DNA >10^5 IU/mL
items.explanation: Antiviral therapy is not recommended; these patients merit continued monitoring.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.question: When can antiviral treatment be discontinued in HBeAg-positive noncirrhotic patients?
items.explanation: Antiviral treatment can be discontinued after HBeAg loss/seroconversion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended duration of consolidation therapy after stopping antiviral treatment in noncirrhotic patients who have achieved HBeAg seroconversion?
items.explanation: The suggested period of consolidation therapy is 12 months with close monitoring.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.question: In the Toronto STOP study, what was the frequency of HBsAg loss in both the stop-treatment and continue-treatment groups?
items.explanation: In the Toronto STOP study, HBsAg loss occurred with equal, small frequencies in both the stop-treatment group (4%) and the continue-treatment group (5%).
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 2)

items.question: According to the RETRACT B study, what was the virologic relapse rate at 48 months after treatment cessation in HBeAg-negative patients without cirrhosis?
items.explanation: In the RETRACT B study, virologic relapse rates were 83.4% at 48 months after treatment cessation in HBeAg-negative patients without cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which factor was identified as the most important predictor of HBsAg loss in the RETRACT B study?
options: HBV DNA level at therapy discontinuation
Quantitative HBsAg level at therapy discontinuation
items.explanation: The most important predictor of HBsAg loss identified in the RETRACT B study was the quantitative HBsAg level at the time therapy was discontinued.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which oral antiviral agent is preferred in patients with creatinine clearance <50 mL/min, reduced bone density, and risk factors for renal injury?
items.explanation: TAF is preferred in patients with creatinine clearance <50 mL/min and other risk factors for renal injury.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following statements about oral agents compared to PEG IFN is true?
options: Oral agents are less well tolerated than PEG IFN.
Oral agents lead to improved histology in 50–90% of patients.
Oral agents are not effective in patients who fail to respond to IFN-based therapy.
items.explanation: Oral agents lead to improved histology in 50–90% of patients and are very well tolerated.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as influencing the choice between TDF and entecavir?
items.explanation: Insurance coverage influences the choice, but patient age is not specifically mentioned as a factor between TDF and entecavir.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which antiviral is considered the current drug of choice for treating hepatitis B during pregnancy?
items.explanation: Tenofovir is preferred due to its safety profile and effectiveness in pregnancy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which antiviral should be avoided during pregnancy due to an increased risk of spontaneous abortion?
items.explanation: Adefovir is associated with an increased risk of spontaneous abortion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: For children aged 2 to <18 years old with HBeAg-positive hepatitis B, under what condition should treatment be recommended?
options: if HBV DNA is detectable and ALT levels are elevated
regardless of HBV DNA or ALT levels
items.explanation: Treatment is recommended if HBV DNA is detectable and ALT levels are elevated.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which antiviral therapy is preferred for patients with HBV-HIV co-infection due to its better safety profile?
items.explanation: TAF (a form of tenofovir) is preferred for HBV-HIV co-infection due to its better safety profile.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)
items.question: Which of the following agents is associated with the highest risk of HBV reactivation?
items.explanation: B cell-depleting agents are associated with the highest risk of HBV reactivation.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)

items.subtopic: POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)
items.question: What is recommended for patients with detectable HBsAg prior to initiating chemotherapy or long-term immunosuppressive regimens?
items.explanation: Preemptive antiviral prophylaxis is recommended for patients with detectable HBsAg.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)

items.subtopic: POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)
items.question: What is the suggested duration of antiviral therapy after chemotherapy for inactive hepatitis B carriers?
items.explanation: A suggested approach is 6 months of antiviral therapy after completion of chemotherapy for inactive hepatitis B carriers.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)

items.question: Which treatment has been conditionally authorized by the European Medicines Agency for HDV infection?
items.explanation: Bulevirtide was conditionally authorized by the European Medicines Agency for HDV treatment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.question: What is the main limitation of standard interferon-alpha therapy in chronic hepatitis D?
options: It has no impact on HBV DNA levels.
It provides only transient reduction in HDV RNA and aminotransferase activity.
It leads to immediate liver failure.
items.explanation: Standard interferon-alpha therapy lowers HDV RNA and aminotransferase activity only transiently during treatment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.question: Which combination therapy showed significant reductions in HDV RNA at the end of 24 weeks?
items.explanation: Bulevirtide combined with tenofovir showed significant reductions in HDV RNA compared to tenofovir monotherapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.question: What is the reported effect of high-dose interferon-alpha therapy on 12-year survival after liver transplantation?
options: No significant change in survival rates.
Decrease in survival rates compared to standard dose.
Increase in survival rates from 31% to 86% with high dose.
Survival rates were identical for both doses.
items.explanation: High-dose interferon-alpha therapy was associated with an increase in 12-year survival after liver transplantation (86 vs 31%).
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.question: What percentage of individuals who experience acute hepatitis C go on to develop chronic infection?
items.explanation: Chronic hepatitis follows acute hepatitis C in 50–70% of cases.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 1)

items.question: Which genetic variation has been identified as distinguishing between responders and nonresponders to IFN-based antiviral therapy for chronic hepatitis C?
items.explanation: Variation in a single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) on chromosome 19, IL28B, distinguishes between responders and nonresponders to IFN-based antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 1)

items.question: What is the likelihood of spontaneous resolution after acute hepatitis C infection for genotype T/T?
items.explanation: The same variants correlated with spontaneous resolution after acute infection: 53% in genotype C/C, 30% in genotype C/T, but only 23% in genotype T/T.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of patients with chronic hepatitis C remain asymptomatic and well compensated over a decade?
items.explanation: Almost 60% of patients with chronic hepatitis C remain asymptomatic and well compensated over the course of a decade.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.question: Which age cohort accounts for three-quarters of the mortality associated with hepatitis C in the United States?
items.explanation: Three-quarters of the mortality associated with hepatitis C occurs in the 'baby boomer' cohort born between 1945 and 1965.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.question: What was the peak year for observed HCV-related mortality in the United States?
items.explanation: The observed HCV-related mortality in the United States peaked in 2013.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.question: Which factor is NOT predictive of eventual outcome in chronic hepatitis C?
options: Presence or absence of jaundice during acute hepatitis
items.explanation: No epidemiologic or clinical features, including severity of acute hepatitis and level of aminotransferase activity, are predictive of eventual outcome in chronic hepatitis C.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.question: Which histologic feature is most predictive of progression to cirrhosis in chronic hepatitis C?
options: Moderate to severe necroinflammatory activity or fibrosis
items.explanation: Progression to cirrhosis is highly likely in patients with moderate to severe necroinflammatory activity or fibrosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 3)

items.question: What is the estimated 10-year survival rate for patients with compensated cirrhosis associated with hepatitis C?
items.explanation: The 10-year survival rate for patients with compensated cirrhosis associated with hepatitis C is close to 80%.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 3)

items.question: Which extrahepatic complication of chronic hepatitis C is linked to cutaneous vasculitis and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis?
items.explanation: Essential mixed cryoglobulinemia is linked to cutaneous vasculitis and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 3)

items.question: Which drug was introduced in 2011 as a protease inhibitor used in combination with PEG IFN and ribavirin for HCV genotype 1?
items.explanation: Both telaprevir and boceprevir were introduced in 2011 as protease inhibitors for HCV genotype 1.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis C

items.question: What was the significant advancement in antiviral therapy for hepatitis C beginning in 2013?
options: Introduction of PEG IFN with ribavirin
Approval of nucleoside analogue polymerase inhibitor sofosbuvir
items.explanation: The field was transformed with the approval of sofosbuvir, a nucleoside analogue polymerase inhibitor.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis C

items.question: As of 2020, what are the characteristics of the recommended combination regimens for hepatitis C treatment?
options: Oral, highly effective (>95%), low-resistance, pangenotypic, well-tolerated, short-duration (8–12 weeks)
Intravenous, moderately effective, high-resistance, genotype-specific, poorly tolerated
Oral, less than 50% effective, high-resistance, pangenotypic, long-duration
items.explanation: The recommended regimens are oral, highly effective (>95%), low-resistance, pangenotypic, well-tolerated, and short-duration (8–12 weeks).
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis C

items.question: What was the sustained virologic response (SVR) rate when subcutaneous IFN-α was administered three times a week for 6 months?
items.explanation: Subcutaneous IFN-α three times a week for 6 months achieved an SVR rate of <10%.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.question: What was the effect of adding ribavirin to IFN-based therapy?
options: It had no significant impact on SVR rates.
It reduced the likelihood of virologic relapse and increased SVR rates to 40%.
It decreased the duration of treatment required for genotypes 1 and 4.
It was effective as a monotherapy.
items.explanation: Adding ribavirin to IFN-based therapy increased the SVR rate to 40% by reducing virologic relapse likelihood.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.question: Which patient variable correlated with higher IFN-based SVRs?
options: Favorable genotype such as genotypes 2 and 3
items.explanation: A favorable genotype, specifically genotypes 2 and 3, correlated with higher IFN-based SVRs.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.question: What is the significance of achieving an early virologic response (EVR) by week 12?
options: It predicts failure to achieve a subsequent SVR.
It indicates a high likelihood of achieving an SVR.
It has no impact on predicting treatment outcomes.
It suggests that the patient will need extended therapy.
items.explanation: Achieving an EVR by week 12 indicates a high likelihood of achieving an SVR.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.question: What was the standard of care for HCV genotypes 2 and 3 until late 2013?
items.explanation: Until late 2013, PEG IFN–ribavirin remained the standard of care for patients with HCV genotypes 2 and 3.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 2)

items.question: Which patient groups were contraindicated for IFN-ribavirin-based therapy?
items.explanation: IFN-ribavirin-based therapy was contraindicated in patients with decompensated liver disease or end-stage renal disease.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 2)

items.question: What is the impact of achieving an SVR on HCV-associated extrahepatic disorders?
options: Has no effect on extrahepatic disorders
Improves conditions such as type 2 diabetes and renal disease
items.explanation: Successful antiviral therapy resulting in an SVR improves HCV-associated extrahepatic disorders like type 2 diabetes and renal disease.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following first-generation protease inhibitors were approved by the FDA in 2011 for treating hepatitis C genotype 1?
items.explanation: Telaprevir and boceprevir were the first-generation protease inhibitors approved by the FDA in 2011 for hepatitis C genotype 1.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.question: What was a necessary component of treatment with first-generation protease inhibitors to reduce relapse rates?
items.explanation: Ribavirin was necessary in combination with protease inhibitors to reduce relapse rates significantly.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.question: What type of therapy did telaprevir-based regimens consist of?
options: Triple therapy followed by dual therapy
items.explanation: Telaprevir-based regimens consisted of periods of triple therapy (protease inhibitor plus PEG IFN plus ribavirin) followed by dual therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.question: Which adverse effect was specifically associated with telaprevir?
items.explanation: Telaprevir was associated with a severe, generalized maculopapular rash in about 6% of treated patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.question: Which factor did NOT contribute to the obsolescence of first-generation protease inhibitors for treating genotype 1 hepatitis C?
options: Amplified intolerability and complexity of regimens
Severe side effects in cirrhotic nonresponders
Rapid progress on next-generation DAA therapy
items.explanation: Improved SVRs with PEG IFN-ribavirin did not contribute to the obsolescence; rather, it was the inferiority compared to protease inhibitors.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 2)

items.question: What was a significant challenge of triple-drug protease inhibitor therapy for hepatitis C?
options: High pill burden and thrice-daily dosing
items.explanation: The high pill burden and the need for thrice-daily dosing were significant challenges.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 2)

items.question: Which adverse outcome was reported in up to 45% of cirrhotic nonresponders treated with first-generation protease inhibitors?
items.explanation: Serious adverse events were reported in up to 45% of cirrhotic nonresponders.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following drug combinations was approved in December 2014 for genotypes 1 and 4?
items.explanation: The combination of ritonavir-boosted paritaprevir, ombitasvir, and dasabuvir was approved in December 2014 for genotypes 1 and 4.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary reason simeprevir was no longer in clinical use?
options: It required PEG IFN and ribavirin for efficacy.
It had many drug-drug interactions and limited efficacy with certain HCV polymorphisms.
It was not effective against genotype 1 of HCV.
It caused severe hepatotoxicity in all patients.
items.explanation: Simeprevir was no longer used due to many drug-drug interactions, restriction to genotype 1, side effects, and limited efficacy with certain polymorphisms.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of sofosbuvir?
options: It requires PEG IFN for all genotypes.
It has a high barrier to resistance and pangenotypic activity.
It is only effective in treatment-naïve patients.
It must be administered twice daily.
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir is characterized by its high potency, high barrier to resistance, and pangenotypic activity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.question: What was a significant limitation of the paritaprevir-ritonavir, ombitasvir, and dasabuvir combination?
options: It could not be used in any genotype.
It required daily injections instead of oral administration.
It was contraindicated in patients with decompensated cirrhosis due to potential hepatotoxicity.
It had no efficacy in treatment-naïve patients.
items.explanation: The combination was contraindicated in patients with decompensated cirrhosis due to potential hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of the paritaprevir-ritonavir, ombitasvir, and dasabuvir regimen compared to later regimens?
options: It requires twice-a-day ribavirin therapy for genotype 1a.
It has higher rates of severe bradycardia when administered with amiodarone.
It is contraindicated in patients without cirrhosis.
It does not provide an option for patients with renal failure.
items.explanation: The regimen required twice-a-day ribavirin therapy for genotype 1a, which was a disadvantage compared to later regimens.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)
items.question: For which genotype was the daclatasvir-sofosbuvir regimen initially approved by the FDA in July 2015?
items.explanation: The daclatasvir-sofosbuvir regimen was initially approved by the FDA for genotype 3.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)
items.question: What was the SVR12 rate for genotype 3 patients with cirrhosis treated with daclatasvir plus sofosbuvir?
items.explanation: The SVR12 rate was 69% for genotype 3 patients with cirrhosis who were prior nonresponders.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.question: Which patient group was shown to achieve an SVR12 rate of 97–100% with sofosbuvir-ledipasvir without the need for therapy extension or ribavirin?
options: Cirrhotic prior nonresponders to IFN-based therapy
items.explanation: In cirrhotic, treatment-naive subjects, SVR12 was achieved in 97–100% without extending therapy or adding ribavirin.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.question: For which genotype did elbasvir-grazoprevir show an SVR12 rate of 100% in clinical trials?
items.explanation: Elbasvir-grazoprevir achieved an SVR12 rate of 100% for genotype 4 in clinical trials.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.question: What is a contraindication when using sofosbuvir-containing regimens?
options: Use in patients with advanced renal failure
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir-containing combinations are contraindicated with the antiarrhythmic agent amiodarone.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.question: Which baseline condition in genotype 1a patients reduces the efficacy of elbasvir-grazoprevir from 99% to 58%?
items.explanation: Baseline NS5A RASs in genotype 1a patients reduce the efficacy of elbasvir-grazoprevir.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for using elbasvir-grazoprevir?
items.explanation: Elbasvir-grazoprevir is contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.question: What should be done if a patient treated with elbasvir-grazoprevir shows ALT elevations exceeding 10-fold?
options: Continue the drug and monitor closely
Increase the dose of the drug
items.explanation: The drug should be stopped for elevations exceeding 10-fold.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a potential side effect seen in patients treated with sofosbuvir-velpatasvir?
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir-velpatasvir can cause mild headache, seen in >10% of patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.question: For which HCV genotypes was the combination of sofosbuvir-velpatasvir approved?
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir-velpatasvir was approved for genotypes 1–6.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following drug combinations should not be administered with amiodarone due to potential serious bradycardia?
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir-containing regimens, including sofosbuvir-velpatasvir and sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir, should not be administered with amiodarone.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.question: What is the recommended treatment duration for sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir in treatment-naïve patients?
items.explanation: The full 12-week course is recommended as the 8-week duration was found to be inferior.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.question: In which patient group is sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir specifically recommended?
options: All patients with chronic hepatitis C
Noncirrhotic patients with prior NS5A inhibitor treatment failure
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir is recommended for noncirrhotic or cirrhotic patients with prior NS5A inhibitor treatment failure.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.question: What is the efficacy of glecaprevir-pibrentasvir in achieving SVR12 for genotype 1, 2, and 4–6?
items.explanation: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir achieves ~99% SVR12 for genotypes 1, 2, and 4–6.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)
items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for using glecaprevir-pibrentasvir?
items.explanation: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir is contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)
items.question: For which patient subgroup is glecaprevir-pibrentasvir considered a preferred treatment?
items.explanation: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir is a preferred treatment for patients with severe renal impairment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)
items.question: What is the recommended duration of glecaprevir-pibrentasvir treatment for noncirrhotic treatment-naive patients?
items.explanation: The recommended duration of glecaprevir-pibrentasvir treatment for noncirrhotic treatment-naive patients is 8 weeks.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended pretreatment evaluation for patients with hepatitis C?
options: Assessment of liver stiffness by imaging
items.explanation: Blood glucose levels are not part of the recommended pretreatment evaluation for hepatitis C.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: What is a crucial reason to determine HCV genotype prior to therapy despite the availability of pangenotypic regimens?
options: To decide on the duration of therapy
Because it affects insurance coverage decisions
It determines if ribavirin is needed
items.explanation: HCV genotype may contribute to decisions about which treatment regimens are indicated and can guide insurance coverage.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: What is a key reason for conducting fibrosis assessment before initiating hepatitis C therapy?
options: To determine the need for liver biopsy
To decide on the antiviral agents and duration of therapy
To assess patient eligibility for vaccination
items.explanation: The presence or absence of cirrhosis or advanced fibrosis determines the treatment options, including the antiviral agents to be used and the duration of therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: Why is screening for HBV infection recommended before initiating DAA therapy in patients with hepatitis C?
options: To prevent severe hepatitis B reactivation during or after treatment
Because it affects the choice of antiviral agents
To determine if ribavirin is needed
items.explanation: Screening for HBV infection is recommended to prevent severe hepatitis B reactivation during and after DAA therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: Which of the following patient groups are considered candidates for antiviral therapy with DAA agents?
options: Patients with chronic hepatitis C and detectable HCV RNA in serum, regardless of aminotransferase levels.
Only patients with elevated aminotransferase levels and advanced liver disease.
Patients with short life expectancies where treating hepatitis C would influence longevity.
Patients who have spontaneously recovered from acute hepatitis C.
items.explanation: All patients with chronic hepatitis C and detectable HCV RNA are candidates for antiviral therapy, regardless of aminotransferase levels.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.question: What is the recommended approach for patients who have not responded to prior DAA therapy containing sofosbuvir?
options: Retreat with a protease inhibitor-containing regimen.
Re-treat with sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir or glecaprevir-pibrentasvir for 12 or 16 weeks, respectively.
Delay treatment until spontaneous recovery is observed.
Use IFN-based therapy as the first line of retreatment.
items.explanation: The recommended approach for nonresponders to prior DAA therapy containing sofosbuvir is re-treatment with specific regimens like sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir or glecaprevir-pibrentasvir.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.question: Which patients should not receive protease inhibitor-containing regimens?
options: Patients with mild fibrosis and normal aminotransferase levels.
Patients who have failed DAA therapy but show no resistance mutations.
items.explanation: Protease inhibitors are contraindicated for patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.question: What is the current recommendation regarding postexposure prophylaxis after HCV exposure?
options: It is recommended for all individuals exposed to HCV.
Only recommended for patients with advanced liver disease.
Recommended only if RAS testing shows susceptibility.
items.explanation: Postexposure prophylaxis after HCV exposure is not currently recommended.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.question: Which antiviral regimen is contraindicated in patients with decompensated cirrhosis due to potential hepatotoxicity?
options: Sofosbuvir combined with an NS5A inhibitor
items.explanation: Protease-inhibitor-containing combinations are associated with potential hepatotoxicity and hepatic decompensation in patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment duration for ribavirin-ineligible patients with decompensated cirrhosis?
items.explanation: For ribavirin-ineligible patients, the duration of therapy should be extended to 24 weeks.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is advised for HCV/HIV co-infected patients with low-level HCV RNA?
items.explanation: For patients with HCV/HIV co-infection and low-level HCV RNA, a full 12 weeks of sofosbuvir-ledipasvir should be given.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.question: In the context of HCV/HIV co-infection, which drug combination should be avoided due to potential toxicity?
items.explanation: In HCV/HIV-infected patients, ribavirin can potentiate the toxicity of didanosine (e.g., lactic acidosis), so this combination should be avoided.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.question: Which of the following DAA combinations is recommended for patients with severe renal impairment?
items.explanation: Both elbasvir-grazoprevir for genotypes 1 and 4 or glecaprevir-pibrentasvir for all genotypes are recommended for severe renal impairment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding DAA use during pregnancy?
options: Ribavirin-containing regimens are safe and recommended.
Sofosbuvir and ledipasvir can be used safely at week 23–24 of gestation.
All DAAs have a pregnancy category B classification.
Screening for HCV infection is not recommended during pregnancy.
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir and ledipasvir were demonstrated to be safe and effective when given at week 23–24 of gestation in a small study.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.question: Which DAA regimen is contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis?
items.explanation: Protease-inhibitor-containing DAA regimens, including elbasvir-grazoprevir, glecaprevir-pibrentasvir, and sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir, are contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.question: What is the goal set by the World Health Organization for reducing global HCV prevalence by 2030?
items.explanation: The WHO has set a goal of a 90% reduction in the global prevalence of HCV infection by 2030.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.question: Which patient group typically requires in-person specialist care for HCV treatment?
options: Complex patients such as those with advanced fibrosis or prior nonresponse to DAA treatment
items.explanation: In-person specialist care is usually not needed except in complex patients such as those with advanced fibrosis, comorbid conditions, and prior nonresponse to DAA treatment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.question: What is one strategy used by successful programs in streamlining HCV treatment?
options: Extensive liver test monitoring during treatment
In-person specialist care for all patients
Minimal monitoring with full course of pills dispensed at the beginning of therapy
Frequent HCV RNA testing during treatment
items.explanation: Successful programs often involve minimal monitoring, dispensing the full course of pills at the beginning of therapy and relying on limited patient contacts.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.question: Which histopathologic lesions are predominantly found in the liver of patients with autoimmune hepatitis (AIH)?
options: Cytotoxic T cells and plasma cells
items.explanation: The liver lesions in AIH are composed predominantly of cytotoxic T cells and plasma cells.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which genetic markers are commonly associated with autoimmune hepatitis (AIH)?
items.explanation: Histocompatibility haplotypes such as HLA-B1, B8, DR3, and DR4 are common in AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which of the following is a proposed mechanism for T cell activation in autoimmune hepatitis (AIH)?
options: Inhibition of regulatory CD4+CD25+ T cells only
items.explanation: Molecular mimicry by cross-reacting antigens is postulated to activate T cells in AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.question: What type of therapy is effective for treating autoimmune hepatitis (AIH)?
items.explanation: Autoimmune hepatitis is responsive to glucocorticoid/immunosuppressive therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is the most common symptom of autoimmune hepatitis (AIH), occurring in approximately 85% of patients?
items.explanation: Fatigue is the most common symptom, occurring in ~85% of AIH patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: In severe cases of AIH, what may be a significant clinical feature?
items.explanation: In severe disease, jaundice may be present.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: Which group of patients with AIH is more likely to have positive lupus erythematosus preparations and other autoimmune features?
items.explanation: Young to middle-aged women with marked hyperglobulinemia and high-titer circulating ANAs are more likely to have these features.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: What is a poor prognostic sign in AIH patients at the time of initial presentation?
items.explanation: The presence of multilobular collapse histologically is a poor prognostic sign.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: Which autoantibody is most characteristically associated with Type I autoimmune hepatitis (AIH)?
items.explanation: Type I AIH is most characteristically associated with antinuclear antibodies (ANAs) in a homogeneous staining pattern.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following autoantibodies is specific to Type II autoimmune hepatitis?
items.explanation: Type II AIH is associated with anti-LKM1, which targets cytochrome P450 2D6.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which histological feature is commonly observed in autoimmune hepatitis but not typically seen in chronic viral hepatitis?
items.explanation: Classic features of AIH include interface hepatitis, which is inflammation extending beyond the portal tracts.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a factor weighing in favor of an AIH diagnosis?
options: Presence of nuclear and smooth-muscle autoantibodies
Characteristic histologic features like interface hepatitis
items.explanation: Predominant alkaline phosphatase elevation weighs against the diagnosis of AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic test is critical to exclude acute viral hepatitis during chronic hepatitis evaluation?
options: Histologic assessment for bile duct injury
items.explanation: Exclusion of acute viral hepatitis with serologic testing is critical.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What distinguishes ICI hepatitis from AIH in terms of autoantibodies?
options: Higher titer ANA in ICI hepatitis
Presence of plasma cell inflammatory infiltrates in ICI hepatitis
Low-titer or absent ANA in ICI hepatitis
High levels of mitochondrial antibodies in ICI hepatitis
items.explanation: Patients with ICI are less likely to have ANA, which, if present, is low-titer and unlikely to have plasma cell inflammatory infiltrates histologically.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition may present with features of chronic hepatitis before neurologic manifestations become apparent?
items.explanation: In adolescence, Wilson disease may present with features of chronic hepatitis long before neurologic manifestations become apparent.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which corticosteroid is commonly used as first-line therapy for autoimmune hepatitis in the United States?
items.explanation: Prednisone is favored by most authorities and commonly used as first-line therapy for autoimmune hepatitis in the United States.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: What is considered a therapeutic response to glucocorticoid treatment in AIH?
options: >50% reduction in ALT by 4 weeks of therapy
>75% reduction in AST by 2 weeks of therapy
Complete normalization of liver enzymes within 1 week
No change in ALT levels after 6 months
items.explanation: A therapeutic response is defined as a >50% reduction in ALT by 4 weeks of therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: When should azathioprine be introduced after starting corticosteroids for AIH?
options: 7–14 days after observing a response to corticosteroids
items.explanation: Azathioprine should be introduced 7–14 days after starting corticosteroids and observing a response.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: What is the likelihood of relapse in AIH patients after completing one course of therapy?
items.explanation: The rate of relapse is 81% after 3 years in AIH patients who have completed one course of therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: Which medication has been shown to achieve remission rates of 95% at 1 year in patients with refractory AIH?
items.explanation: Rituximab has been shown to achieve remission rates of 95% at 1 year in patients with refractory AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.question: What is a significant risk associated with the use of tacrolimus in treating AIH?
items.explanation: Tacrolimus requires careful trough monitoring and comes with a risk of nephrotoxicity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.question: When should liver transplantation be considered for patients with severe AIH?
options: If bilirubin does not improve after 2 weeks of therapy
items.explanation: Failure of bilirubin to improve after 2 weeks of therapy should prompt early consideration for liver transplantation.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.subtopic: Chronic Hepatitis | CLASSIFICATION OF CHRONIC HEPATITIS
items.statement: Clinical features alone are sufficient to characterize all categories of chronic hepatitis.
items.explanation: Neither clinical nor histologic features alone are sufficient; a combination is required.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis | CLASSIFICATION OF CHRONIC HEPATITIS

items.subtopic: Chronic Hepatitis | CLASSIFICATION OF CHRONIC HEPATITIS
items.statement: Patients with chronic hepatitis may have overlapping types of liver injury.
items.explanation: Overlapping types, such as viral and steatotic liver injury, are not infrequent.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis | CLASSIFICATION OF CHRONIC HEPATITIS

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION BY CAUSE | CLASSIFICATION BY GRADE
