items.statement: Chronic hepatitis can be graded as mild, moderate, or severe based on histologic features.
items.explanation: The presence and degree of histologic activity features allow chronic hepatitis to be graded as mild, moderate, or severe.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLASSIFICATION BY CAUSE | CLASSIFICATION BY GRADE

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION BY CAUSE | CLASSIFICATION BY GRADE
items.statement: Autoimmune hepatitis is caused by the hepatitis B virus.
items.explanation: Autoimmune hepatitis is not caused by the hepatitis B virus; it includes several subcategories based on serologic distinctions.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLASSIFICATION BY CAUSE | CLASSIFICATION BY GRADE

items.statement: Hepatitis E can cause chronic liver disease in immunosuppressed hosts.
items.explanation: Hepatitis E can indeed cause chronic liver disease in immunosuppressed individuals, such as after liver transplantation.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLASSIFICATION BY STAGE

items.statement: The METAVIR scoring system categorizes hepatic fibrosis on a scale from 0 to 6.
items.explanation: The METAVIR scoring system categorizes hepatic fibrosis on a scale from 0 to 4.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLASSIFICATION BY STAGE

items.statement: Co-infection with HIV increases the risk of liver-related mortality in patients with hepatitis B.
items.explanation: HIV co-infection has been shown to accelerate progression and increase liver-related mortality.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.statement: The transition from HBeAg-positive to HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B indicates an acceleration of disease progression.
items.explanation: This transition signals a slowing of disease progression.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.statement: HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B is more common than HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B in Mediterranean and European countries.
items.explanation: HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B is indeed more prevalent in these regions compared to HBeAg-positive.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.statement: Inactive carriers of HBV have detectable levels of HBeAg.
items.explanation: Inactive carriers are characterized by undetectable or minimal levels of HBeAg.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B
items.statement: All forms of chronic hepatitis B can be progressive.
items.explanation: Progression to cirrhosis is more likely in severe cases, but all forms can progress, especially with active HBV replication.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B
items.statement: Hepatocellular carcinoma risk decreases when high-level HBV DNA falls spontaneously over time.
items.explanation: The risk of hepatocellular carcinoma is highest with continued, high-level HBV replication and lower when HBV DNA levels fall.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B

items.statement: Lamivudine therapy at daily doses of 100 mg for 48–52 weeks suppressed HBV DNA to undetectable levels in ~40% of HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B patients.
items.explanation: Lamivudine therapy did suppress HBV DNA to undetectable levels in approximately 40% of HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Standard IFN therapy is still the preferred treatment for chronic hepatitis B.
items.explanation: Standard IFN has been supplanted by long-acting PEG IFN and newer antivirals, making it no longer the preferred treatment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Lamivudine therapy resulted in frequent HBsAg losses during the first year.
items.explanation: Losses of HBsAg were few during the first year of lamivudine therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Combination therapy with lamivudine and IFN showed benefits over monotherapy in treatment-naïve patients.
items.explanation: Trials failed to show a benefit of combination therapy over lamivudine monotherapy for either treatment-naïve patients or prior IFN nonresponders.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Lamivudine has been shown to be teratogenic in rodents.
items.explanation: The safety of lamivudine during pregnancy has not been established, but it is not teratogenic in rodents.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.statement: Adefovir dipivoxil is recommended as first-line therapy for treatment-naïve patients.
items.explanation: Adefovir is no longer recommended as first-line therapy due to being supplanted by more potent agents like tenofovir.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.statement: Telbivudine is recommended as first-line therapy for chronic hepatitis B.
items.explanation: Telbivudine is neither recommended as first-line therapy nor used widely due to its inferior resistance and safety profile.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 4)

items.statement: Resistance mutations after 2 years of telbivudine treatment occurred in up to 22%.
items.explanation: The passage states that resistance mutations after 2 years of telbivudine treatment occurred in up to 22%.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 4)

items.statement: PEG IFN-α2a is approved for hepatitis B treatment in the United States.
items.explanation: PEG IFN-α2a was approved in the United States for hepatitis B in 2005.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: The combination of PEG IFN and lamivudine showed no benefit compared to PEG IFN monotherapy at six months after therapy.
items.explanation: Neither the combination arm nor the lamivudine monotherapy arm demonstrated any benefit compared to the PEG IFN monotherapy arms 6 months after therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Entecavir has a high barrier to resistance and no resistance mutations were encountered in treatment-naïve patients during 96 weeks.
items.explanation: No resistance mutations were encountered in previously treatment-naïve, entecavir-treated patients during 96 weeks.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: PEG IFN monotherapy resulted in HBsAg loss in more than 10% of patients at the end of therapy.
items.explanation: HBsAg loss occurred in only 4% of patients treated with PEG IFN monotherapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Entecavir can be used as monotherapy for HIV/HBV co-infected persons.
items.explanation: Entecavir has low-level antiviral activity against HIV and cannot be used alone in HIV/HBV co-infection.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Tenofovir alafenamide requires a higher dose than tenofovir disoproxil fumarate to achieve similar antiviral potency.
items.explanation: TAF is more potent at a lower dose due to targeted delivery, requiring only 25 mg compared to TDF's 300 mg.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Combination therapies of oral nucleoside/nucleotide agents have shown enhanced virologic efficacy over monotherapy.
items.explanation: No enhancement in efficacy was observed with combination therapies compared to the more potent individual drug.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.statement: For patients with creatinine clearances <15 mL/min, neither TDF nor TAF is recommended.
items.explanation: Both drugs are not recommended for such low levels of renal function.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.statement: RNA interference therapies can permanently eliminate HBsAg.
items.explanation: HBsAg reappears over time after RNAi therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.statement: Toll-like receptor agonists have been universally successful in achieving HBsAg loss.
items.explanation: Most immunomodulatory approaches, including toll-like receptor agonists, have been abandoned for lack of efficacy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.statement: Antiviral treatment should be considered for HBeAg-positive patients with ALT >2× the upper limit of normal and HBV DNA levels >2 × 10⁴ IU/mL.
items.explanation: Treatment is recommended by AASLD for these criteria.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: For HBeAg-negative patients, antiviral therapy can be stopped after HBsAg loss.
items.explanation: Stopping therapy in this group is considered only after HBsAg loss, which remains exceedingly rare.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: For patients with compensated cirrhosis, antiviral therapy is recommended regardless of HBeAg status and ALT as long as HBV DNA is detectable.
items.explanation: Antiviral therapy is indeed recommended for patients with compensated cirrhosis regardless of HBeAg status and ALT if HBV DNA is detectable.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: In the Toronto STOP study, a higher percentage of patients in the stop-treatment group experienced HBsAg loss compared to those in the continue-treatment group.
items.explanation: HBsAg loss occurred with equal frequencies in both groups (4% for stop-treatment and 5% for continue-treatment) in the Toronto STOP study.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Oral agents are associated with more intensive clinical and laboratory monitoring than PEG IFN.
items.explanation: Oral agents do not require cumbersome laboratory monitoring, unlike PEG IFN.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Several studies confirm a differential effect of TDF on long-term HCC risk without confounding factors.
items.explanation: Adjusting for confounding factors often eliminates the benefit of TDF over entecavir.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: PEG IFN should be used in patients with compensated or decompensated cirrhosis.
items.explanation: PEG IFN is contraindicated due to the risk of life-threatening side effects like sepsis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Preemptive treatment with lamivudine can reduce the risk of hepatitis B reactivation in patients undergoing chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Lamivudine is effective in reducing the risk of HBV reactivation when used preemptively.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)
items.statement: Chronic HDV infection following acute co-infection with HBV and HDV occurs at a higher rate than the chronicity of acute hepatitis B.
items.explanation: The rate of chronic HDV infection following acute co-infection is no higher than the rate of chronicity of acute hepatitis B.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)

items.subtopic: POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)
items.statement: Chronic hepatitis B plus D has similar clinical and laboratory features to chronic hepatitis B alone.
items.explanation: Except for severity, chronic hepatitis B plus D has similar clinical and laboratory features to those seen in chronic hepatitis B alone.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 2) | CHRONIC HEPATITIS D (DELTA HEPATITIS)

items.statement: Nucleoside analogue antiviral agents for hepatitis B are effective against hepatitis D.
items.explanation: None of the nucleoside analogue antiviral agents for hepatitis B are effective in hepatitis D.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.statement: Bulevirtide blocks the NTCP receptor, preventing hepatocyte entry of both HBV and HDV.
items.explanation: Bulevirtide is an entry inhibitor that blocks the NTCP receptor.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.statement: Most cases of hepatitis C are identified initially in symptomatic patients with a history of acute hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Most cases of hepatitis C are identified initially in asymptomatic patients who have no history of acute hepatitis C.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 1)

items.statement: Approximately one-third of patients with chronic hepatitis C have normal or near-normal aminotransferase activity.
items.explanation: Approximately one-third of patients with chronic hepatitis C have normal or near-normal aminotransferase activity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 1)

items.statement: The annual rate of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in cirrhotic patients with hepatitis C is 5-10%.
items.explanation: The annual rate of HCC in cirrhotic patients with hepatitis C is 1–4%, not 5-10%.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.statement: Direct-acting antiviral agents have contributed to a decline in HCV-related mortality.
items.explanation: The introduction of highly effective DAA agents has been associated with declining death rates related to HCV infection.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.statement: Jaundice is a common symptom in chronic hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Jaundice is rare in patients with chronic hepatitis C.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 3)

items.statement: The peak prevalence of hepatitis C was estimated to have occurred in 2015.
items.explanation: Weighted by the concentration of chronic hepatitis C in the baby boomer generation, the peak prevalence was estimated to have occurred in 2015.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 3)

items.statement: The introduction of PEG IFN with ribavirin marked the beginning of antiviral therapy for hepatitis C.
items.explanation: PEG IFN with ribavirin was part of the early therapeutic armamentarium introduced after interferon-α.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis C

items.statement: Sofosbuvir is a protease inhibitor used in combination regimens for HCV genotype 1.
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir is a nucleoside analogue polymerase inhibitor, not a protease inhibitor.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis C

items.statement: Ribavirin is effective as a monotherapy for chronic hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Ribavirin, when used alone, was ineffective; it increased SVR rates only when combined with IFN.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.statement: Patients who achieve a rapid virologic response (RVR) have a very high likelihood of achieving an SVR.
items.explanation: HCV RNA becoming undetectable within 4 weeks (RVR) correlates with a very high likelihood of achieving an SVR.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.statement: Successful treatment with DAAs has eliminated the need for continued clinical monitoring in patients with advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Even after successful DAA treatment, there is a reduced but persistent risk of liver-related death and HCC, necessitating continued clinical monitoring.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 2)

items.statement: PEG IFN-ribavirin nonresponders can now be retreated with DAAs to achieve SVR rates comparable to treatment-naïve persons.
items.explanation: Nonresponders to PEG IFN-ribavirin can be successfully treated with DAAs, achieving SVR rates similar to those in treatment-naïve individuals.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 2)

items.statement: First-generation protease inhibitors could be administered with medications that induce CYP3A4.
items.explanation: Both telaprevir and boceprevir inhibit CYP3A4, so they cannot be used with medications that induce or are dependent on CYP3A4 for elimination.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.statement: SVR rates were highest among cirrhotic prior null responders treated with first-generation protease inhibitors.
items.explanation: SVRs were lowest in cirrhotic prior null responders, not highest.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.statement: First-generation protease inhibitors were recommended therapy for hepatitis C beyond 2013.
items.explanation: These drugs became obsolete after a brief stint as recommended therapy from 2011 to 2013.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 2)

items.statement: First-generation protease inhibitors required PEG IFN injections and ribavirin.
items.explanation: The therapy included the need for PEG IFN injections and ribavirin, contributing to its complexity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.statement: Sofosbuvir is used in combination with ribavirin for all HCV genotypes.
items.explanation: Sofosbuvir can be used without ribavirin, especially in IFN-free regimens.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.statement: Simeprevir was effective in treatment-naïve and treatment-experienced patients without cirrhosis.
items.explanation: The combination of simeprevir with sofosbuvir showed high SVR rates in these patient groups.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)
items.statement: The daclatasvir-sofosbuvir regimen is recommended by the AASLD for genotype 1.
items.explanation: The daclatasvir-sofosbuvir regimen is no longer recommended by the AASLD.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)
items.statement: The daclatasvir-sofosbuvir regimen was effective in treatment-naïve patients with genotype 3 and cirrhosis.
items.explanation: The combination was effective, achieving a 94% SVR12 rate in treatment-naïve patients with genotype 3 and cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.statement: Sofosbuvir-ledipasvir was initially recommended for patients with advanced renal failure.
items.explanation: Initially, sofosbuvir-ledipasvir was not recommended in patients with advanced renal failure.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.statement: Elbasvir-grazoprevir is effective for genotype 6 without any modifications.
items.explanation: Only 80% of patients with genotype 6 achieved SVR12, indicating it's not fully effective without modifications.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.statement: Baseline NS5A RAS testing is required before starting elbasvir-grazoprevir treatment.
items.explanation: Elbasvir-grazoprevir requires baseline NS5A RAS testing.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.statement: Ribavirin is required for all patients treated with sofosbuvir-velpatasvir.
items.explanation: Ribavirin is not required except in decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.statement: Baseline RASs influence the responsiveness to sofosbuvir-velpatasvir.
items.explanation: Baseline RASs do not influence responsiveness to the sofosbuvir-velpatasvir combination.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.statement: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir is contraindicated in patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Like all protease inhibitor-containing combinations, glecaprevir-pibrentasvir is contraindicated in patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)
items.statement: Successful DAA therapy has been associated with a reduction in HCC.
items.explanation: DAA therapy is linked to a reduction in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)
items.statement: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir should be taken on an empty stomach.
items.explanation: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir should be taken with food.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)

items.statement: Noninvasive measures of fibrosis have replaced liver biopsy in almost all cases for assessing histologic grade and stage.
items.explanation: Noninvasive approaches like imaging and FIB-4 score have supplanted histology in most cases.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.statement: Patients with low-level fibrosis at baseline do not require posttreatment HCC surveillance after achieving SVR.
items.explanation: In patients with low-level fibrosis, achievement of SVR allows cessation of HCC surveillance.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.statement: Patients with persistently normal aminotransferase activity do not progress histologically and are not candidates for antiviral therapy.
items.explanation: Even patients with persistently normal aminotransferase activity respond to antiviral therapy and are considered candidates.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.statement: Delaying treatment of acute hepatitis C for spontaneous recovery is currently recommended.
items.explanation: Delaying the initiation of therapy in acute hepatitis C to allow for spontaneous recovery is no longer recommended.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.statement: Patients with decompensated cirrhosis are contraindicated for protease-inhibitor-containing regimens due to potential hepatotoxicity.
items.explanation: Protease-inhibitor-containing combinations have been associated with potential hepatotoxicity and hepatic decompensation in patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: DAAs are not recommended for use immediately after liver transplantation.
items.explanation: DAAs are effective and can be used immediately after liver transplantation, either for donor or recipient HCV infection.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.statement: Ribavirin is recommended for use during pregnancy in HCV treatment.
items.explanation: Ribavirin is contraindicated during pregnancy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.statement: Sofosbuvir-containing regimens are approved for use in patients with severe renal impairment.
items.explanation: Based on clinical trials, sofosbuvir-containing regimens were approved by the FDA for patients with severe renal impairment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.statement: The U.S. goal for HCV prevalence reduction by 2030 is set at 90%.
items.explanation: The U.S. goal is an 80% reduction in the prevalence of HCV infection by 2030.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.statement: AIH has a strong female predominance, with women accounting for approximately 75% of all cases.
items.explanation: AIH indeed shows a strong female predominance, with women making up about 75% of cases.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.statement: The precise triggering factors and genetic influences in autoimmune hepatitis are completely defined.
items.explanation: The precise triggering factors, genetic influences, and mechanisms involved in AIH remain incompletely defined.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Cytotoxic T lymphocytes are implicated in the pathogenesis of autoimmune hepatitis.
items.explanation: CD4+ T lymphocytes can become sensitized to hepatocyte membrane proteins and destroy liver cells.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Cirrhosis at presentation is more common in African Americans than in whites in North America.
items.explanation: The passage states that cirrhosis at presentation is indeed more common in African Americans.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: In AIH, serum bilirubin levels are always elevated.
items.explanation: Many patients with AIH have normal serum bilirubin levels.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Anti-LKM3 antibodies are associated with Type II autoimmune hepatitis.
items.explanation: Anti-LKM3, directed against uridine diphosphate glucuronyltransferases, can be seen in patients with type II AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Granulomas are a common histological finding in autoimmune hepatitis.
items.explanation: Bile duct injury and granulomas are uncommon in AIH, although some overlap with primary biliary cholangitis exists.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Elevated aminotransferase levels are required for the diagnosis of AIH.
items.explanation: Elevated aminotransferase levels are indeed required for the diagnosis of AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Patients with ICI hepatitis typically have high-titer ANA.
items.explanation: Patients with ICI are less likely to have ANA, and if present, it is low-titer.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Budesonide is the preferred first-line corticosteroid for all presentations of AIH.
items.explanation: Prednisone or prednisolone are favored as first-line treatments; budesonide is an alternative for mild cases.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.statement: Serum aminotransferase levels alone reliably indicate recovery in individual AIH patients.
items.explanation: Improvements in AST and ALT alone are not reliable markers of recovery; histologic improvement is more indicative.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.statement: Recurrence of AIH in the new liver post-transplantation occurs in more than 50% of cases.
items.explanation: Recurrence of AIH in the new liver occurs rarely, but in as many as 35–40% of cases.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with AIH should be vaccinated against hepatitis A and B before starting immunosuppressive therapy if possible.
items.explanation: Vaccinations against hepatitis A and B are recommended ideally before beginning immunosuppressive therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 2)

items.assertion: The degree of HBV replication is more important than histology alone in assessing chronic hepatitis B.
items.reason: HBV DNA levels correlate with the level of liver injury and risk of progression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.assertion: Spontaneous HBeAg loss occurs more frequently in patients with genotype C.
items.reason: The systematic review found spontaneous HBeAg loss to be less frequent in patients with genotype C.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B can experience episodic reactivation of liver disease.
items.reason: This is reflected in fluctuating levels of aminotransferase activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.assertion: The onset of chronic hepatitis B is always acute.
items.reason: Chronic disease follows failure to resolve clinically apparent acute hepatitis B in most cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the onset tends to be insidious, not always acute.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 2)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B
items.assertion: Antiviral therapy for hepatitis B has evolved rapidly since the mid-1990s.
items.reason: The sensitivity of tests for HBV DNA has improved, allowing more accurate measurement and comparison in clinical trials.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the improvement in test sensitivity is a contributing factor to the evolution of antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS B (part 3) | Chronic Hepatitis B

items.assertion: Standard IFN therapy was effective in patients with high-level HBV DNA.
items.reason: Responsiveness to IFN was higher in patients with low-level HBV DNA and substantial ALT elevations.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because standard IFN therapy was not effective for patients with high-level HBV DNA, whereas the reason correctly states that responsiveness was higher in those with low-level HBV DNA.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Lamivudine therapy improved histology and prevented progression to cirrhosis.
items.reason: Lamivudine was shown to suppress HBV DNA by a median of ~5.5 log10 copies/mL in HBeAg-positive chronic hepatitis B.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but suppression of HBV DNA is not the direct explanation for improved histology and prevention of cirrhosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Lamivudine is no longer recommended as first-line therapy for hepatitis B.
items.reason: It has been eclipsed by more potent antivirals with superior resistance profiles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why lamivudine is no longer recommended as first-line therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B can achieve an HBeAg response to nucleoside analogue therapy.
items.reason: These patients lack precore or core-promoter HBV mutations and have HBeAg.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because these patients cannot achieve an HBeAg response due to the presence of mutations.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.assertion: Adefovir dipivoxil is less effective than lamivudine in suppressing HBV DNA.
items.reason: Adefovir was not as potent as the other approved oral agents and did not suppress HBV DNA as rapidly or uniformly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.assertion: Lamivudine therapy for hepatitis B increased liver transplantation referrals.
items.reason: Referral of patients with HBV-associated end-stage liver disease for liver transplantation fell by ~30% after lamivudine introduction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; lamivudine therapy actually decreased referrals.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.assertion: Telbivudine is generally well tolerated.
items.reason: It was associated with a low frequency of asymptomatic creatine kinase elevations and very low frequency of myopathy and peripheral neuropathy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why telbivudine is generally well tolerated.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · HISTORICAL, EARLY-GENERATION ANTIVIRAL AGENTS (part 4)

items.assertion: PEG IFN monotherapy should be considered first-line therapy for HBeAg-reactive chronic hepatitis B.
items.reason: A finite, 1-year course of PEG IFN results in a higher rate of sustained response than oral nucleoside/nucleotide analogue therapy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the comparison is confounded by the indefinite extension of oral agents after 1 year.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Resistance is a significant issue with PEG IFN therapy.
items.reason: The risk of resistance is much lower with new nucleoside analogues compared to PEG IFN.
items.explanation: Resistance is not an issue during PEG IFN therapy, making the assertion false.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Entecavir is considered a first-line drug for chronic hepatitis B.
items.reason: Its high potency and barrier to resistance make it superior to other treatments.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why entecavir is a first-line drug.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: PEG IFN therapy should be continued if HBsAg levels do not fall within the first 12–24 weeks.
items.reason: Continuation is recommended to achieve long-term suppression of HBV DNA.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; PEG IFN therapy should be discontinued if HBsAg levels do not fall.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate is the preferred first-line therapy for chronic hepatitis B.
items.reason: It has a high barrier to resistance and favorable long-term safety profile.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the preference.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.assertion: Entecavir is recommended for patients with lamivudine-resistant hepatitis B.
items.reason: It has a lower resistance rate compared to tenofovir disoproxil fumarate.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because entecavir is not recommended due to higher resistance rates; the reason is also false.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 3)

items.assertion: Switching from a resistant drug to a new antiviral is preferred.
items.reason: The current generation of antivirals has a high barrier to resistance, making monotherapy with the rescue drug effective.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.assertion: Combination of entecavir and tenofovir is ineffective in patients with multidrug resistance.
items.reason: This combination has been shown to be highly effective in suppressing HBV DNA and overcoming drug resistance.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 4)

items.assertion: Bulevirtide has not met with success in treating HBV mono-infection.
items.reason: It decreases new cccDNA production by blocking the receptor for HBV on cell membranes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.assertion: Combining immune modulators with direct antiviral agents will definitely improve curative prospects for hepatitis B.
items.reason: Current treatments have not yet achieved sustained HBsAg loss from HBV infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as it's uncertain whether combinations will improve prospects, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE TREATMENT OPTIONS (part 5)

items.assertion: Patients with compensated cirrhosis and detectable HBV DNA should receive antiviral treatment.
items.reason: Treatment is universally recommended for patients with compensated cirrhosis and detectable HBV DNA.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Antiviral therapy is recommended for HBeAg-positive patients with ALT ≤2× the upper limit of normal.
items.reason: These patients are likely to have sustained responses and require multiyear therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; antiviral therapy is not recommended currently for these patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Indefinite treatment is recommended for most HBeAg-negative patients unless they experience HBsAg loss.
items.reason: HBsAg loss can be achieved in a small fraction, and although some may not require reintroduction of therapy short-term, the approach has limited enthusiasm.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why indefinite treatment is recommended for most HBeAg-negative patients unless they experience HBsAg loss.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Entecavir and tenofovir are preferred for treating hepatitis B in patients with cirrhosis.
items.reason: They have a very favorable resistance profile.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the preference.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Lamivudine is an acceptable first-line drug for treating hepatitis B during pregnancy.
items.reason: It has no teratogenicity and is widely used during pregnancy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; lamivudine is not considered a first-line drug despite its safety.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · POPULATIONS WITH UNIQUE TREATMENT CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: High-dose interferon-alpha therapy provides sustained loss of HDV replication in up to 50% of patients.
items.reason: High-dose IFN-$\alpha$ (9 million units three times a week) for 12 months was reported to be associated with clinical improvement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.assertion: Combining PEG IFN with nucleoside analogues is more effective than PEG IFN alone.
items.reason: Adding ribavirin or oral nucleoside agents to PEG IFN was not found to be more effective than PEG IFN monotherapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Chronic Hepatitis D

items.assertion: Continued clinical monitoring and antiviral therapy are indicated for patients with normal aminotransferase activity.
items.reason: Histologic progression is rare among patients with persistent normal aminotransferase activity sustained over ≥5–10 years.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why continued clinical monitoring and antiviral therapy are indicated.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 1)

items.assertion: Chronic hepatitis C is more likely to progress in patients with older age and longer duration of infection.
items.reason: Duration of infection appears to be one of the most important factors influencing progression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why chronic hepatitis C is more likely to progress in these patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.assertion: The severity of acute hepatitis predicts the eventual outcome of chronic hepatitis C.
items.reason: Severity of acute hepatitis does not predict the eventual outcome.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because no clinical features, including severity of acute hepatitis, are predictive of eventual outcome.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CHRONIC HEPATITIS C (part 2)

items.assertion: Absence of HCV RNA 12 weeks after completion of therapy is the current standard for SVR.
items.reason: Most relapses occur within the first 12 weeks after treatment, making this a reliable marker for SVR.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.assertion: IFN gene variants have no impact on IFN responsiveness in patients with genotype 1.
items.reason: Genome-wide association studies discovered IFN gene variants that significantly impacted IFN responsiveness.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because IFN gene variants do impact IFN responsiveness; the reason is true.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · THE INTERFERON ERA (1991–2011) (part 1)

items.assertion: First-generation protease inhibitors were replaced by more effective all-oral regimens.
items.reason: These agents represented a breakthrough in the treatment of chronic hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not why they were replaced; rather, it explains their initial significance.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.assertion: Resistance-associated substitutions could be identified in a substantial proportion of protease inhibitor nonresponders.
items.reason: Wild-type HCV reemerged in almost all cases within 1.5–2 years.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why resistance-associated substitutions were identified.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 1)

items.assertion: First-generation protease inhibitors were no longer recommended after 2013.
items.reason: The rapid progress on next-generation and all-oral DAA therapy made them obsolete.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why first-generation protease inhibitors became obsolete.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · FIRST-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS (2011–2013) (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.assertion: Sofosbuvir is a pangenotypic nucleoside polymerase inhibitor.
items.reason: It has efficacy in all genotypes (1–6).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why sofosbuvir is considered pangenotypic.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)
items.assertion: The combination of paritaprevir-ritonavir, ombitasvir, and dasabuvir is suitable for patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reason: It was contraindicated in patients with decompensated cirrhosis due to potential hepatotoxicity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the combination is contraindicated in these patients.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)
items.assertion: The daclatasvir-sofosbuvir regimen is no longer recommended by the AASLD.
items.reason: Newer combination DAAs have supplanted it due to better efficacy and fewer side effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the regimen is no longer recommended.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRAL COMBINATIONS OF SECOND-GENERATION PROTEASE INHIBITORS, FIRST-GENERATION POLYMERASE INHIBITORS, AND FIRST-GENERATION NS5A INHIBITORS (2014–2015) (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.assertion: Sofosbuvir-ledipasvir is contraindicated with amiodarone.
items.reason: All sofosbuvir-containing regimens can be associated with severe bradycardia in patients taking the antiarrhythmic agent amiodarone, especially along with beta blockers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)
items.assertion: Baseline NS5A RASs in genotype 1a patients do not affect the efficacy of elbasvir-grazoprevir.
items.reason: NS5A polymorphisms may include resistance-associated substitutions that reduce drug efficacy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because baseline NS5A RASs in genotype 1a patients do reduce the efficacy of elbasvir-grazoprevir.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.assertion: Sofosbuvir-velpatasvir is effective in both treatment-naïve and treatment-experienced patients.
items.reason: Clinical trials showed high SVR12 rates across different patient categories.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)
items.assertion: Elbasvir-grazoprevir is suitable for patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reason: It is contraindicated in such cases due to potential hepatotoxicity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly states why it's not suitable.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.assertion: Sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir is recommended for patients with prior NS5A inhibitor treatment failure.
items.reason: This combination is effective in a small subset of otherwise refractory patients, including those who had failed previous NS5A inhibitor therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why sofosbuvir-velpatasvir-voxilaprevir is recommended for this group.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)
items.assertion: Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir achieves higher SVR12 rates in patients with prior protease inhibitor and NS5A inhibitor treatment.
items.reason: SVR12 rates are reduced by baseline RASs, particularly those reflecting NS5A resistance.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Glecaprevir-pibrentasvir achieves lower SVR12 rates in patients with prior treatment involving both protease inhibitors and NS5A inhibitors.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)
items.assertion: Early and broad implementation of DAAs has contributed to the decline in HCV-related deaths since 2013.
items.reason: DAAs have been instrumental in reducing the hepatitis C-associated disease burden.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CURRENT FIRST-LINE COMBINATIONS OF DIRECT-ACTING ANTIVIRALS (part 4)

items.assertion: The goal of hepatitis C treatment is to achieve sustained virologic response (SVR).
items.reason: Successful therapy reduces the risk of HCC substantially but does not eliminate it.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.assertion: Pretreatment evaluation for hepatitis C should include HCV RNA and genotype testing.
items.reason: These tests help determine the duration of therapy and guide insurance coverage decisions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

items.assertion: Patients with advanced liver disease should be prioritized for antiviral therapy.
items.reason: Early treatment in patients with advanced liver disease merits a high priority due to potential benefits.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early treatment is prioritized.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.assertion: Protease inhibitors are recommended for patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.reason: Protease inhibitors have been shown to be effective in all stages of liver disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because protease inhibitors are contraindicated for decompensated cirrhosis, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · INDICATIONS FOR ANTIVIRAL THERAPY

items.assertion: Patients with high MELD scores should receive DAA therapy before liver transplantation.
items.reason: Postponing DAA therapy until after liver transplantation is advised for patients with high-MELD-score HCV-associated, end-stage liver disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; postponement of DAA therapy is recommended for patients with high MELD scores.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Active injection drug use is a contraindication to HCV treatment.
items.reason: A randomized, controlled clinical trial showed that sofosbuvir and velpatasvir were highly successful in PWID with SVR12 rates of 74–76%.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; active injection drug use is no longer considered a contraindication to treatment.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.assertion: Pregnant women should be routinely treated with DAAs for HCV.
items.reason: DAAs are classified as pregnancy category B and have been shown to be safe in small studies.
items.explanation: DAAs are not indicated routinely during pregnancy unless the benefit outweighs potential fetal risk.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · TREATMENT OF UNIQUE POPULATIONS (part 2) | CHOOSING AMONG AVAILABLE TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.assertion: The age distribution of AIH is bimodal.
items.reason: AIH has one peak in the early decades (age <30) and a second peak in middle to late years (>60).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the bimodal age distribution of AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · APPROACHES TO STEAMLINING DAA TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.assertion: Autoimmune disorders occur with increased frequency in patients who have AIH.
items.reason: This is due to the shared genetic predisposition and immune dysregulation.
items.explanation: Other autoimmune disorders are more common in AIH patients, likely due to shared genetic factors.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: The presence of circulating autoantibodies is essential for the pathogenesis of AIH.
items.reason: Autoantibodies provide helpful diagnostic markers but their role in pathogenesis is not established.
items.explanation: While prevalent, the involvement of autoantibodies in AIH pathogenesis has not been confirmed.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · IMMUNOPATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: The course of AIH can be variable.
items.reason: Patients may experience spontaneous remissions and exacerbations, affecting the disease's progression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the variability in AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Laboratory features of AIH always correlate with clinical severity.
items.reason: Liver biochemical tests are invariably abnormal in AIH.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because laboratory abnormalities may not correlate with clinical severity.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: The presence of SLA autoantibodies is associated with a worse prognosis in AIH.
items.reason: SLA autoantibodies are found in a subset of patients with Type I AIH who have clinical features similar to or more severe than other patients with the same type.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of SLA autoantibodies is indeed associated with a worse prognosis.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Autoimmune biliary disorders may overlap with AIH features.
items.reason: Overlap syndromes are difficult to categorize, and often response to therapy may be the distinguishing factor that establishes the diagnosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Cryptogenic cirrhosis can be distinguished from AIH by histologic assessment.
items.reason: Histologic assessments are usually sufficient to allow these entities to be distinguished from AIH.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not specifically explain how cryptogenic cirrhosis is distinguished from AIH.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Azathioprine should be introduced after corticosteroids to maintain remission in AIH.
items.reason: Azathioprine acts as a steroid-sparing agent, reducing the need for long-term high-dose steroids.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; azathioprine is introduced to reduce corticosteroid dependence.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)

items.assertion: Mycophenolate mofetil has been proven superior to azathioprine in randomized controlled trials.
items.reason: MMF is considered a second-line agent if azathioprine is not tolerated or ineffective.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; MMF's superiority over azathioprine has not been proven in randomized controlled trials.
items.reference: Chronic Hepatitis · Autoimmune Hepatitis (part 1)
